Base directory for this skill: /Users/alisha/Projects/marvin/plugin/skills/marvin

# Marvin

`marvin` now supports a smaller workflow:

- `plan`
- `plan-tui`
- `investigate`
- `investigate-tui`
- `implement`
- `work`
- `wt`
- `clean`
- `status`
- `today`
- `memory`

Use these commands directly. Do not reference older hook-based collaboration commands.

## Commands

| Command | What it does |
| --- | --- |
| `/marvin plan` | Run the autonomous multi-agent plan loop and write a plan doc plus timeline. |
| `/marvin plan-tui` | Configure and launch `marvin plan` from the TUI. |
| `/marvin investigate` | Run the autonomous investigation loop and write findings plus timeline. |
| `/marvin investigate-tui` | Configure and launch `marvin investigate` from the TUI. |
| `/marvin implement [spec-path] --agents ...` | Create one implementation branch/worktree per agent. If `spec-path` is omitted, it resolves the current repo's plan collab. |
| `/marvin work` | Print or launch an implementation prompt. In an implement worktree it uses the managed spec; otherwise it resolves a plan collab in the current repo. |
| `/marvin wt` | Create, list, and remove ad hoc worktrees under the configured worktree root, grouped by repo name. |
| `/marvin clean [collab-id...]` | Remove completed collabs, or remove specific collab IDs explicitly. |
| `/marvin status [collab-id]` | Show collabs for the current repo by default, `--all` for every saved collab, or one collab in detail. |
| `/marvin today` | Hierarchical "what did I do" view over the last 24h. Joins JSON snapshots with the memory graph; pass `--since 168h` for "this week" or `--all` for cross-repo. |
| `/marvin memory serve` | Boot the memory sidecar (idempotent). `--stop` shuts it down. |
| `/marvin memory status` | Report sidecar PID, schema version, episode count, and disabled state. |
| `/marvin memory search --topic "<keywords>"` | Query prior decisions ranked by relevance. JSON output matches the `<prior_decisions>` block. |
| `/marvin memory claims --topic "<keywords>"` | Search prior investigation claims (capability #4). Surfaces "established <date>" hints. |
| `/marvin memory fixes --topic "<keywords>"` | Search prior (Pattern → Fix → File) triples (capability #5). |
| `/marvin memory list` | List decisions/plans for the current repo's group. `--scope global` opts into cross-repo entries. |
| `/marvin memory show <id>` | Print full node + edges for one entity. |
| `/marvin memory record-decision --title "..." --rationale "..."` | Capture a sub-decision an agent reached mid-turn (vs Marvin's post-consensus write). |
| `/marvin memory record-fix --pr <num>` | Capture a (Pattern → Fix → File) triple from a merged PR (uses `gh pr view`). |
| `/marvin memory forget <id>` | Remove an entity. `--purge` also drops its supersede chain. |

## Typical flow

Before kicking off a fresh plan, check whether marvin (or another agent on
this machine) has already solved something similar. Run the `entire:recall`
skill with a one-line description of the task. If it surfaces a relevant
prior session, fold the decisions and remaining gaps into the new prompt
via the `marvin-plan-prompt` skill — that converts "we've been here before"
into a starting point instead of a duplicate plan loop.

Create a plan:

```bash
marvin plan --topic "Should we replace Postgres with FoundationDB?"
```

Launch implementation worktrees:

```bash
marvin implement --agents "claude-code,codex"
```

Inside one of those worktrees:

```bash
marvin work
```

If you stay in the main repo after planning, `/marvin work` also works there. If multiple plan collabs exist for the repo, marvin prompts you to choose one.

## Live overview

`plan`, `plan-tui`, `investigate`, and `investigate-tui` all use the same live overview screen when running interactively:

- the main document
- the timeline
- live transcripts
- stance history

Use the TUI when the user wants a form-based setup flow instead of flags.

## Memory

Marvin records decision provenance from accepted plans and prepends a
`<prior_decisions>` block to plan, investigate, and work prompts so
future runs reuse rationale instead of re-litigating it. The block is
treated as untrusted historical context — it cannot deliver
instructions to the agent.

Agents can also actively query and write memory mid-turn. The plan,
investigate, and work prompts advertise these tools so claude-code,
codex, and gemini-cli all reach the same store identically — no MCP
wiring required:

```bash
marvin memory search --topic "<keywords>" --json   # query prior decisions
marvin memory record-decision --title "..." --rationale "..."  # capture a sub-decision
```

Other notes:

- Memory is per-repo by default (group ID derived from the canonical
  remote URL). `--scope global` is the cross-repo opt-in.
- Run `marvin memory serve` once to boot the sidecar; it survives
  marvin invocations and self-terminates after 30 minutes idle.
- The backend is graphiti's MCP server + FalkorDB, run via Docker.
  `marvin memory serve` runs `docker compose up -d`; data persists in
  `~/.marvin/memory/falkordb/`. Restarts, idle timeouts, and reboots
  do not lose the graph.
- If Docker is missing or the MCP endpoint doesn't come up within
  90 seconds, marvin disables memory for the rest of the run and
  continues normally.
- Set `MARVIN_MEMORY=off` to skip every memory call for one process.
- See `internal/memory/README.md` for schema + the pinned runtime.

## Where docs live

By default, plan and investigation docs land **outside** the repo so
they survive repo deletion and aren't part of any project's source tree:

- Plans: `~/.marvin/plans/<repo-name>/<slug>.md` (and `<slug>-timeline.md`)
- Findings: `~/.marvin/investigations/<repo-name>/<slug>.md`

Pass `--output <path>` to override. A relative path is resolved against
the repo root (so `--output docs/plans/foo.md` writes inside the repo,
matching the old behavior).

## Notes

- `marvin implement` is setup-only. Use normal git workflows to compare, merge, and clean up implementation branches.
- All commands output JSON by default unless the command is intentionally interactive.
- Supported agent types are `claude-code`, `codex`, and `gemini-cli`.

# Plan: add a skill that teaches dev that is new to using entire how to use cli. should get overview of different workflows etc...

**Status:** approved
**Authors:** claude-code, codex
**Created:** 2026-05-07

## Problem / Motivation

A developer who has just installed the Entire CLI (or just run
`npx skills add https://github.com/entireio/skills --all`) and asks their agent
something open-ended like "I'm new to Entire, what can it do?", "give me a
tour", or "teach me how to use this" gets generic prose at best. None of the
existing five skills (`search`, `explain`, `what-happened`, `session-handoff`,
`session-to-skill`) trigger on intro-style phrasing because they are all
task-specific.

The README has a "Quick Start" with example prompts, but it is read by humans
on github.com, not by the agent at the moment the user asks. There is no
agent-invokable entry point that:

1. detects whether `entire` is installed, authenticated, and enabled in the
   current repo,
2. teaches the actual `entire` CLI commands the user has on their machine
   (not a curated subset, not the five existing skills) by introspecting the
   live `entire --help` surface and presenting them as workflow-shaped
   recipes ("if you need to do X, run `entire …`"),
3. demos one workflow against the user's actual repo so the tour ends with
   evidence,
4. mentions the focused skills only as deeper drill-down options for the
   workflows the CLI already supports.

Without this entry point, new users either bounce to docs.entire.io (losing
the agent context), or get a freeform answer that drifts from the canonical
commands, or get taught commands that no longer exist in their installed CLI.

## Goals

1. Add one new skill, `skills/getting-started/SKILL.md`, that triggers on
   intro-style prompts ("I'm new to Entire", "tour", "teach me Entire", "what
   can Entire do", "getting started", "show me how this works", "intro to
   Entire").
2. The skill is **CLI-driven, not skill-driven**: every command it teaches
   comes from running `entire --help` (and `entire <subcommand> --help` /
   `entire help <subcommand>`) at invocation time and parsing the
   `Available Commands` block. It must never hardcode a fixed command list.
   If a future CLI release adds, removes, or renames a command, the next
   invocation of the skill teaches the new surface automatically.
3. The skill is **workflow-shaped**: it presents commands as "if you want
   to do X → run `entire …`" pairs, not as a catalog. It maps the
   subcommands discovered in step 2 onto a small fixed set of user goals
   (set up, see what happened, find prior work, understand a change,
   undo / recover, summarize, share / collaborate, troubleshoot,
   advanced). Subcommands that do not match any goal are listed as
   "Other commands available in your CLI: …" so nothing is silently
   hidden, but they are not promoted.
4. The skill **never teaches an experimental command without flagging
   it**. After enumerating subcommands, it inspects each one's help text
   for the markers `experimental`, `beta`, `alpha`, `preview`, or
   `labs` (case-insensitive, whole-word) and tags any matching command
   `(experimental)` in its workflow lines. If the CLI ever introduces a
   top-level group named `labs` (it does not today), the same rule
   applies: list its members under "Other commands" with the
   `(experimental)` tag rather than under the main workflow goals.
5. The skill detects the user's current state (`entire` installed?
   inside a git repo? `entire enable` run in this repo? supported
   agents installed? authenticated for cloud search? any usable local
   or remote history available?) and adapts its branch accordingly —
   it does not lecture a user who is already set up, and it does not
   skip past setup for a user who has not run `entire enable`.
6. When usable history exists, the skill demos exactly one concrete
   result against the user's repo using a CLI command that exists in
   their installed help surface. When history does not exist yet, the
   skill ends with the exact next capture step needed to create that
   first result.
7. The skill mentions each focused skill (`search`, `explain`,
   `what-happened`, `session-handoff`, `session-to-skill`) at most
   once, as a "deeper drill-down" hint inline with the matching
   workflow goal — not as the primary content. Removing all skill
   mentions must still leave the response useful on its own.
8. Update `README.md` so the new skill appears in Quick Start, the
   "What skills help agents do" table, and the "Included skills" list.
   While touching that section, fix the current README drift by
   ensuring the already-shipped `session-to-skill` skill is listed
   there too.
9. Ship the skill through every packaging surface that actually
   versions this repo today: the six versioned manifests plus the
   pinned tag in `.opencode/INSTALL.md`, then verify discovery with the
   validation command in `CONTRIBUTING.md`.

## Non-Goals

- No changes to the `entire` CLI itself. This plan lives entirely in the
  `entireio/skills` repo.
- Not a replacement for `docs.entire.io` — the skill links to docs for depth,
  it does not duplicate them.
- Not a setup automation. The skill instructs the user to run `entire enable`,
  `entire login`, and `entire agent add <name>` interactively; it does not run
  those for them, because they need the user's choices (telemetry, agent
  selection, checkpoint remote).
- No new CLI subcommand, no new MCP tool, no plugin manifest changes beyond
  what skill discovery requires.
- Not a session-recap skill. If the user wants to summarize a past session,
  they want `session-handoff` or `session-to-skill`; this skill points there
  rather than reimplementing it.
- Not a video/asset deliverable. Adding a recording to the README is out of
  scope for this PR; the existing four skills have recordings, so a follow-up
  recording can be added later without changing the skill itself.

## Proposal

### Skill name and location

- Path: `skills/getting-started/SKILL.md`
- Skill `name`: `getting-started`
- Invoked as `/getting-started` (Claude Code), and as `entire:getting-started`
  in cross-agent contexts where the plugin namespace is exposed.

`getting-started` was chosen over `tour`, `intro`, or `entire-tour` because it
matches the prompts a new user actually types, is the conventional name for
this category of skill across plugins, and avoids colliding with this repo's
earlier `code-tour` / `project-overview` concept, which was about onboarding to
an unfamiliar repository rather than onboarding to Entire itself.

### Frontmatter

```yaml
---
name: getting-started
description: >
  Use when the user is new to Entire and wants an overview of what the CLI
  does, an interactive tour of the main workflows, or a guided first run.
  Triggers on phrases like "I'm new to Entire", "give me a tour", "teach me
  Entire", "what can Entire do", "getting started", "show me how this works",
  "intro to Entire", "how do I use entire", and "where do I start".
---
```

The description is intentionally trigger-heavy because these phrases are how
new users actually ask, and because the existing five skills are all
task-shaped and will not match intro phrasing.

### Response header

Following the convention used by every other skill in this repo (`Entire
Search:`, `Entire Explain:`, `Entire What Happened:`, `Entire Session
Handoff:`, `Entire Session To Skill:`), this skill begins its first successful
response with:

```
Entire Getting Started:
```

followed by a blank line, then the content. `Entire Tour:` was rejected because
it reads like a continuation of the older `code-tour` repo-overview work; the
header should line up with the actual skill name. Header rules match the
existing skills:

- Apply the header to the first response of the invocation only.
- Do not include the header on early-exit error responses (CLI not installed,
  not a git repo, etc.).
- Do not re-print the header on follow-up turns within the same invocation.

### Workflow

The skill body is structured as a preflight → state-detection →
**discovery from `entire --help`** → workflow-shaped tour → live-demo flow.
Each section names exact commands so the agent does not improvise, and the
discovery step is what makes the skill self-updating.

**1. Preflight and detect state.** Run the first set in parallel and do not
show raw output:

```bash
git rev-parse --is-inside-work-tree
entire version
entire status --json
entire auth status
entire session current --json
```

If `entire status --json` says Entire is enabled, gather the local-history
probes in parallel:

```bash
entire checkpoint list --no-pager
entire dispatch --local --all-branches --since 30d
```

Use those commands to derive state with these rules:

- `git rev-parse` fails → tell the user Entire works inside a git repository,
  and stop without the header.
- `entire version` fails / command not found → tell the user how to install
  the CLI (link to `https://docs.entire.io/cli/install`), and stop without the
  header.
- `entire status --json` says `enabled: false` → enter the **Setup tour**
  branch.
- `entire status --json` says `enabled: true` and `agents: []` → enter the
  **Agent install** branch.
- `entire status --json` says enabled and agents are installed, but the repo has
  no usable history yet → enter the **First-capture tour** branch.
- `entire status --json` says enabled and agents are installed, and the repo has
  usable history → enter the **Workflow tour** branch.

`usable history` means any one of:

- `entire checkpoint list --no-pager` shows at least one checkpoint on the
  current branch (i.e. its output does not include the literal line
  `No checkpoints found on this branch.`),
- `entire session current --json` returns a session object whose `checkpoints`
  field (or equivalent count) is `> 0`,
- `entire dispatch --local --all-branches --since 30d` exits 0 and prints a
  Markdown summary that contains at least one section heading
  (e.g. a `## ` line) — i.e. it is not just a "no recent activity" stub.

Do **not** use `entire checkpoint list` as the sole gate. It is branch-local.
In this repo right now, `entire checkpoint list --no-pager` on
`learn-entire-cli` prints `No checkpoints found on this branch` even though
`entire dispatch --local --all-branches --since 30d` returns a real Markdown
report summarizing recent Entire history across local branches. The skill
must treat that combination as the **Workflow tour** state, not the
**First-capture tour** state.

**2. Discover the live CLI surface (every successful invocation).** Before
presenting any workflow content, run:

```bash
entire --help
```

Parse the `Available Commands:` block: every line in that block has the
shape `^  <name>\s{2,}<short description>$`. The list of names produced by
that parse is the set of **top-level command groups** the skill is allowed
to teach. For each name in that list, also run:

```bash
entire help <name>
```

(or `entire <name> --help` if `entire help` fails) so the skill has the
short description, longer help text, and any subcommands. Parse each
top-level help page's own `Available Commands:` block the same way so the
skill can promote concrete command paths like `agent add`,
`session current`, `session list`, `checkpoint explain`, and
`checkpoint search` instead of only opaque group names. Then, for each
explicit subcommand path discovered from those pages that may be rendered
to the user, also run that leaf help page (`entire <name> <subcommand>
--help`, or `entire help <name> <subcommand>` if supported) so the skill
has the exact help text for the concrete command path it is about to
teach. Fan out these independent help lookups in parallel rather than
sequentially so the skill does not turn the tour into a slow help crawl.
The allowed
rendered command surface for one invocation is therefore:

- every top-level command listed in `entire --help`, plus
- every explicit subcommand path listed in the help output of those
  top-level commands.

Cache these outputs for the duration of one invocation only — the next
invocation re-reads them so a CLI upgrade between invocations is picked up
automatically.

The skill then derives, in memory, a structured map shaped roughly like:

```text
{ path, summary, helpText, parent, subcommands, requiresLogin, isExperimental, goals }
```

`requiresLogin` is true if the help text for that exact rendered command
path says authentication is required (for example `entire checkpoint search
--help` currently says `Requires authentication via 'entire login'`).
This check must use the leaf command's own help text, not only its parent
group help page; `entire checkpoint --help` does not carry the auth
requirement for `entire checkpoint search`.
Rendered workflow lines and "Other commands available in your CLI" entries
append `(login required)` when this is true, so logged-out users can see
which workflows are cloud-backed before they try them.

`isExperimental` is true if `helpText` for that exact rendered command path
contains a whole-word
case-insensitive match for `experimental`, `beta`, `alpha`, `preview`,
or `labs`. `goals` is a small set of fixed labels the skill assigns by
matching the command path and short description against the table below;
the names on the right are the labels used in the rendered tour, not the
literal command names.

| If the CLI exposes…              | the skill places it under goal… |
| --------------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
| `enable`, `disable`, `agent add`, `agent list`, `login`, `logout`, `auth`, `configure` | Set up |
| `status`, `session current`, `session list`, `session info`, `activity` | See what's going on |
| `checkpoint search` | Find prior work |
| `checkpoint explain`, `checkpoint list` | Understand a change |
| `checkpoint rewind`, `clean` | Undo / recover |
| `dispatch` | Summarize / share |
| `doctor` | Troubleshoot |
| anything else (including unknown names from a future release) | Other commands available in your CLI |

The mapping table itself is part of the skill body. The skill
**never invents** command paths that are not in the discovered help
surface; if a goal has no matching command path in this CLI version,
that goal is omitted from the rendered tour rather than filled with a
guess. If a discovered command path does not match any goal (e.g. a
hypothetical future top-level `entire trace` or a nested
`entire doctor trace` that the workflow table does not promote), the
skill lists it under "Other commands available in your CLI" with its
short description verbatim and no extra wording.

**3. Setup tour branch.** Tell the user, in 5–8 lines:
- what `entire enable` does (turns on session capture and checkpointing
  in this repo),
- to run it now: `entire enable`,
- to log in if they want cloud search: `entire login`,
- that they will be asked which agent to install hooks for, and the
  next step is `entire agent add <agent-name>`,
- then re-invoke this skill so the tour can continue with real state.

Do not run `entire enable` for them — the command is interactive and
asks about telemetry, agent selection, and checkpoint remote. Names
used here (`enable`, `login`, `agent add`) are confirmed against the
discovery output before being printed; if any of them is missing from
the live `entire --help` (e.g. on a stripped-down distribution), drop
the missing line from this branch instead of printing a dead
instruction.

**4. Agent install branch.** Detect the user's agent from environment
if possible (`CLAUDE_CODE`, `CODEX_*`, `CURSOR_*`, `OPENCODE_*`, etc.;
fall back to asking). Tell the user to run `entire agent add
<agent-name>` and explain that this installs the hooks that capture
sessions and create checkpoints on commit. Mention `entire agent list`
to see what is supported. (`agent` and its `add` / `list` subcommands
must appear in the discovered surface; if they do not, fall back to
"Run `entire help` to see how to install agent hooks in your version.")

**5. First-capture tour branch.** Entire is enabled and a supported
agent is installed, but there is not enough captured history yet to
demo the workflows concretely. Tell the user that checkpoints are
created automatically when their agent runs and commits, and that to
see the system work end-to-end they should:
- run a small task with their agent (any task, even "say hello in a
  comment"),
- commit the change,
- optionally run `entire login` if they want cloud search as part of
  the tour,
- come back and re-invoke this skill — there will be captured history
  to demo.

Do not fabricate a checkpoint or run a placeholder task on the user's
behalf.

**6. Workflow tour branch (the main path).** Render the goals → command
recipe. The lines below are the **template**; the actual rendered text
is built from the discovery output, so any goal whose required command
path is absent is dropped, any command path marked `requiresLogin`
gets a `(login required)` suffix, and any command marked
`isExperimental` gets an `(experimental)` suffix. The deeper-drill-down skill names in
parentheses are mentioned at most once each and only on the line where
the workflow already exists in the CLI:

```text
Here is what your installed Entire CLI can do (entire <CLI version>):

- Set up                  →  entire enable / entire agent add <name> / entire login
- See what's going on     →  entire status / entire session current / entire session list / entire activity
- Find prior work         →  entire checkpoint search "<query>" (login required)      (deeper: /search)
- Understand a change     →  entire checkpoint explain <id|sha>      (deeper: /explain, /what-happened)
- Undo / recover          →  entire checkpoint rewind  /  entire clean
- Summarize / share       →  entire dispatch --since 7d              (deeper: /session-handoff)
- Troubleshoot            →  entire doctor

Other commands available in your CLI:
- <name>: <short description verbatim from `entire --help`>
- ...
```

The skill prefers the canonical command spellings advertised in
`entire --help`. `entire search` exists today as a hidden alias of
`entire checkpoint search`; the rendered tour uses
`entire checkpoint search` because that is the form the live help
actually advertises. If a future CLI release promotes `search` to a
top-level command (it would then appear in `Available Commands`), the
discovery step picks that up and the rendered line uses the shorter
form on its own. Likewise, auth-gated commands are tagged from the live
help text for that exact command path, not from a hardcoded list.

Then **demo exactly one** concrete Entire result, using a valid command
path in this order. Each candidate is only attempted if its command was
present in the discovery output:

- If `entire auth status` says the user is logged in **and**

```bash

```

  If that search returns at least one hit, summarize the top hit as

  If it returns zero hits, do **not** show an empty result; fall

- Otherwise, if `entire checkpoint list --no-pager` shows at least one
  checkpoint on the current branch, expand the newest checkpoint with

```bash

```

  Summarize that checkpoint as the demo and point out that `/explain`

- Otherwise, use the local cross-branch summary command:

```bash

```

  Summarize one recent thread of work and frame it as "this is the

End with a one-liner that points at `docs.entire.io/cli` for deeper

### Failure modes

The skill must distinguish and report each of these states explicitly:

- `entire` not installed → "The Entire CLI is required but not installed.
  Install it from https://docs.entire.io/cli/install and try again."
  No header.
- Current directory is not a git repo → "Entire works inside a git
  repository. Run `git init` first or `cd` into one, then re-invoke this
  skill." No header.
- `entire status` works but says disabled → enter the Setup tour branch
  (this is success, header applies).
- `entire login` required for cloud search → mention it in the tour but do
  not block local-only tours on it. Search is auth-gated; `status`,
  `session current`, `checkpoint list`, and `dispatch --local` still give the
  user value without logging in.
- Any other CLI error → report the exact error in one short excerpt and
  suggest re-running with `--help` or filing a `doctor` report
  (`entire doctor`). No header.

68	## Problem / Motivation
69	
70	A developer who has just installed the Entire CLI (or just run
71	`npx skills add https://github.com/entireio/skills --all`) and asks their agent
72	something open-ended like "I'm new to Entire, what can it do?", "give me a
73	tour", or "teach me how to use this" gets generic prose at best. None of the
74	existing five skills (`search`, `explain`, `what-happened`, `session-handoff`,
75	`session-to-skill`) trigger on intro-style phrasing because they are all
76	task-specific.
77	
78	The README has a "Quick Start" with example prompts, but it is read by humans
79	on github.com, not by the agent at the moment the user asks. There is no
80	agent-invokable entry point that:
81	
82	1. detects whether `entire` is installed, authenticated, and enabled in the
83	   current repo,
84	2. teaches the actual `entire` CLI commands the user has on their machine
85	   (not a curated subset, not the five existing skills) by introspecting the
86	   live `entire --help` surface and presenting them as workflow-shaped
87	   recipes ("if you need to do X, run `entire …`"),
88	3. demos one workflow against the user's actual repo so the tour ends with
89	   evidence,
90	4. mentions the focused skills only as deeper drill-down options for the
91	   workflows the CLI already supports.
92	
93	Without this entry point, new users either bounce to docs.entire.io (losing
94	the agent context), or get a freeform answer that drifts from the canonical
95	commands, or get taught commands that no longer exist in their installed CLI.
96	
97	## Goals
98	
99	1. Add one new skill, `skills/getting-started/SKILL.md`, that triggers on
100	   intro-style prompts ("I'm new to Entire", "tour", "teach me Entire", "what
101	   can Entire do", "getting started", "show me how this works", "intro to
102	   Entire").
103	2. The skill is **CLI-driven, not skill-driven**: every command it teaches
104	   comes from running `entire --help` (and `entire <subcommand> --help` /
105	   `entire help <subcommand>`) at invocation time and parsing the
106	   `Available Commands` block. It must never hardcode a fixed command list.
107	   If a future CLI release adds, removes, or renames a command, the next
108	   invocation of the skill teaches the new surface automatically.
109	3. The skill is **workflow-shaped**: it presents commands as "if you want
110	   to do X → run `entire …`" pairs, not as a catalog. It maps the
111	   subcommands discovered in step 2 onto a small fixed set of user goals
112	   (set up, see what happened, find prior work, understand a change,
113	   undo / recover, summarize, share / collaborate, troubleshoot,
114	   advanced). Subcommands that do not match any goal are listed as
115	   "Other commands available in your CLI: …" so nothing is silently
116	   hidden, but they are not promoted.
117	4. The skill **never teaches an experimental command without flagging
118	   it**. After enumerating subcommands, it inspects each one's help text
119	   for the markers `experimental`, `beta`, `alpha`, `preview`, or
120	   `labs` (case-insensitive, whole-word) and tags any matching command
121	   `(experimental)` in its workflow lines. If the CLI ever introduces a
122	   top-level group named `labs` (it does not today), the same rule
123	   applies: list its members under "Other commands" with the
124	   `(experimental)` tag rather than under the main workflow goals.
125	5. The skill detects the user's current state (`entire` installed?
126	   inside a git repo? `entire enable` run in this repo? supported
127	   agents installed? authenticated for cloud search? any usable local
128	   or remote history available?) and adapts its branch accordingly —
129	   it does not lecture a user who is already set up, and it does not
130	   skip past setup for a user who has not run `entire enable`.
131	6. When usable history exists, the skill demos exactly one concrete
132	   result against the user's repo using a CLI command that exists in
133	   their installed help surface. When history does not exist yet, the
134	   skill ends with the exact next capture step needed to create that
135	   first result.
136	7. The skill mentions each focused skill (`search`, `explain`,
137	   `what-happened`, `session-handoff`, `session-to-skill`) at most
138	   once, as a "deeper drill-down" hint inline with the matching
139	   workflow goal — not as the primary content. Removing all skill
140	   mentions must still leave the response useful on its own.
141	8. Update `README.md` so the new skill appears in Quick Start, the
142	   "What skills help agents do" table, and the "Included skills" list.
143	   While touching that section, fix the current README drift by
144	   ensuring the already-shipped `session-to-skill` skill is listed
145	   there too.
146	9. Ship the skill through every packaging surface that actually
147	   versions this repo today: the six versioned manifests plus the
148	   pinned tag in `.opencode/INSTALL.md`, then verify discovery with the

151	## Non-Goals
152	
153	- No changes to the `entire` CLI itself. This plan lives entirely in the
154	  `entireio/skills` repo.
155	- Not a replacement for `docs.entire.io` — the skill links to docs for depth,
156	  it does not duplicate them.
157	- Not a setup automation. The skill instructs the user to run `entire enable`,
158	  `entire login`, and `entire agent add <name>` interactively; it does not run

170	## Proposal
171	
172	### Skill name and location
173	
174	- Path: `skills/getting-started/SKILL.md`
175	- Skill `name`: `getting-started`
176	- Invoked as `/getting-started` (Claude Code), and as `entire:getting-started`
177	  in cross-agent contexts where the plugin namespace is exposed.
178	
179	`getting-started` was chosen over `tour`, `intro`, or `entire-tour` because it
180	matches the prompts a new user actually types, is the conventional name for
181	this category of skill across plugins, and avoids colliding with this repo's
182	earlier `code-tour` / `project-overview` concept, which was about onboarding to
183	an unfamiliar repository rather than onboarding to Entire itself.
184	
185	### Frontmatter
186	
187	```yaml
188	---
189	name: getting-started
190	description: >
191	  Use when the user is new to Entire and wants an overview of what the CLI
192	  does, an interactive tour of the main workflows, or a guided first run.
193	  Triggers on phrases like "I'm new to Entire", "give me a tour", "teach me
194	  Entire", "what can Entire do", "getting started", "show me how this works",
195	  "intro to Entire", "how do I use entire", and "where do I start".
196	---
197	```
198	
199	The description is intentionally trigger-heavy because these phrases are how
200	new users actually ask, and because the existing five skills are all
201	task-shaped and will not match intro phrasing.
202	
203	### Response header
204	
205	Following the convention used by every other skill in this repo (`Entire
206	Search:`, `Entire Explain:`, `Entire What Happened:`, `Entire Session
207	Handoff:`, `Entire Session To Skill:`), this skill begins its first successful
208	response with:
209	
210	```
211	Entire Getting Started:
212	```
213	
214	followed by a blank line, then the content. `Entire Tour:` was rejected because
215	it reads like a continuation of the older `code-tour` repo-overview work; the
216	header should line up with the actual skill name. Header rules match the
217	existing skills:
218	
219	- Apply the header to the first response of the invocation only.
220	- Do not include the header on early-exit error responses (CLI not installed,
221	  not a git repo, etc.).
222	- Do not re-print the header on follow-up turns within the same invocation.
223	
224	### Workflow
225	
226	The skill body is structured as a preflight → state-detection →
227	**discovery from `entire --help`** → workflow-shaped tour → live-demo flow.
228	Each section names exact commands so the agent does not improvise, and the
229	discovery step is what makes the skill self-updating.
230	
231	**1. Preflight and detect state.** Run the first set in parallel and do not
232	show raw output:
233	
234	```bash
235	git rev-parse --is-inside-work-tree
236	entire version
237	entire status --json
238	entire auth status
239	entire session current --json
240	```
241	
242	If `entire status --json` says Entire is enabled, gather the local-history
243	probes in parallel:
244	
245	```bash
246	entire checkpoint list --no-pager
247	entire dispatch --local --all-branches --since 30d
248	```
249	
250	Use those commands to derive state with these rules:
251	
252	- `git rev-parse` fails → tell the user Entire works inside a git repository,
253	  and stop without the header.
254	- `entire version` fails / command not found → tell the user how to install
255	  the CLI (link to `https://docs.entire.io/cli/install`), and stop without the
256	  header.
257	- `entire status --json` says `enabled: false` → enter the **Setup tour**
258	  branch.
259	- `entire status --json` says `enabled: true` and `agents: []` → enter the
260	  **Agent install** branch.
261	- `entire status --json` says enabled and agents are installed, but the repo has
262	  no usable history yet → enter the **First-capture tour** branch.
263	- `entire status --json` says enabled and agents are installed, and the repo has
264	  usable history → enter the **Workflow tour** branch.
265	
266	`usable history` means any one of:
267	
268	- `entire checkpoint list --no-pager` shows at least one checkpoint on the
269	  current branch (i.e. its output does not include the literal line
270	  `No checkpoints found on this branch.`),
271	- `entire session current --json` returns a session object whose `checkpoints`
272	  field (or equivalent count) is `> 0`,
273	- `entire dispatch --local --all-branches --since 30d` exits 0 and prints a
274	  Markdown summary that contains at least one section heading
275	  (e.g. a `## ` line) — i.e. it is not just a "no recent activity" stub.
276	
277	Do **not** use `entire checkpoint list` as the sole gate. It is branch-local.
278	In this repo right now, `entire checkpoint list --no-pager` on
279	`learn-entire-cli` prints `No checkpoints found on this branch` even though
280	`entire dispatch --local --all-branches --since 30d` returns a real Markdown
281	report summarizing recent Entire history across local branches. The skill
282	must treat that combination as the **Workflow tour** state, not the
283	**First-capture tour** state.
284	
285	**2. Discover the live CLI surface (every successful invocation).** Before
286	presenting any workflow content, run:
287	
288	```bash
289	entire --help
290	```
291	
292	Parse the `Available Commands:` block: every line in that block has the
293	shape `^  <name>\s{2,}<short description>$`. The list of names produced by
294	that parse is the set of **top-level command groups** the skill is allowed
295	to teach. For each name in that list, also run:
296	
297	```bash
298	entire help <name>
299	```
300	
301	(or `entire <name> --help` if `entire help` fails) so the skill has the
302	short description, longer help text, and any subcommands. Parse each
303	top-level help page's own `Available Commands:` block the same way so the
304	skill can promote concrete command paths like `agent add`,
305	`session current`, `session list`, `checkpoint explain`, and
306	`checkpoint search` instead of only opaque group names. Then, for each
307	explicit subcommand path discovered from those pages that may be rendered
308	to the user, also run that leaf help page (`entire <name> <subcommand>
309	--help`, or `entire help <name> <subcommand>` if supported) so the skill
310	has the exact help text for the concrete command path it is about to
311	teach. Fan out these independent help lookups in parallel rather than
312	sequentially so the skill does not turn the tour into a slow help crawl.
313	The allowed
314	rendered command surface for one invocation is therefore:
315	
316	- every top-level command listed in `entire --help`, plus
317	- every explicit subcommand path listed in the help output of those
318	  top-level commands.
319	
320	Cache these outputs for the duration of one invocation only — the next
321	invocation re-reads them so a CLI upgrade between invocations is picked up
322	automatically.
323	
324	The skill then derives, in memory, a structured map shaped roughly like:
325	
326	```text
327	{ path, summary, helpText, parent, subcommands, requiresLogin, isExperimental, goals }
328	```
329	
330	`requiresLogin` is true if the help text for that exact rendered command
331	path says authentication is required (for example `entire checkpoint search
332	--help` currently says `Requires authentication via 'entire login'`).
333	This check must use the leaf command's own help text, not only its parent
334	group help page; `entire checkpoint --help` does not carry the auth
335	requirement for `entire checkpoint search`.
336	Rendered workflow lines and "Other commands available in your CLI" entries
337	append `(login required)` when this is true, so logged-out users can see
338	which workflows are cloud-backed before they try them.
339	
340	`isExperimental` is true if `helpText` for that exact rendered command path
341	contains a whole-word
342	case-insensitive match for `experimental`, `beta`, `alpha`, `preview`,
343	or `labs`. `goals` is a small set of fixed labels the skill assigns by
344	matching the command path and short description against the table below;
345	the names on the right are the labels used in the rendered tour, not the
346	literal command names.
347	
348	| If the CLI exposes…              | the skill places it under goal… |
349	| --------------------------------- | ------------------------------- |
350	| `enable`, `disable`, `agent add`, `agent list`, `login`, `logout`, `auth`, `configure` | Set up |
351	| `status`, `session current`, `session list`, `session info`, `activity` | See what's going on |
352	| `checkpoint search` | Find prior work |
353	| `checkpoint explain`, `checkpoint list` | Understand a change |
354	| `checkpoint rewind`, `clean` | Undo / recover |
355	| `dispatch` | Summarize / share |
356	| `doctor` | Troubleshoot |
357	| anything else (including unknown names from a future release) | Other commands available in your CLI |
358	
359	The mapping table itself is part of the skill body. The skill
360	**never invents** command paths that are not in the discovered help
361	surface; if a goal has no matching command path in this CLI version,
362	that goal is omitted from the rendered tour rather than filled with a
363	guess. If a discovered command path does not match any goal (e.g. a
364	hypothetical future top-level `entire trace` or a nested
365	`entire doctor trace` that the workflow table does not promote), the
366	skill lists it under "Other commands available in your CLI" with its
367	short description verbatim and no extra wording.
368	
369	**3. Setup tour branch.** Tell the user, in 5–8 lines:
370	- what `entire enable` does (turns on session capture and checkpointing
371	  in this repo),
372	- to run it now: `entire enable`,
373	- to log in if they want cloud search: `entire login`,
374	- that they will be asked which agent to install hooks for, and the
375	  next step is `entire agent add <agent-name>`,
376	- then re-invoke this skill so the tour can continue with real state.
377	
378	Do not run `entire enable` for them — the command is interactive and
379	asks about telemetry, agent selection, and checkpoint remote. Names
380	used here (`enable`, `login`, `agent add`) are confirmed against the
381	discovery output before being printed; if any of them is missing from
382	the live `entire --help` (e.g. on a stripped-down distribution), drop
383	the missing line from this branch instead of printing a dead
384	instruction.
385	
386	**4. Agent install branch.** Detect the user's agent from environment
387	if possible (`CLAUDE_CODE`, `CODEX_*`, `CURSOR_*`, `OPENCODE_*`, etc.;
388	fall back to asking). Tell the user to run `entire agent add
389	<agent-name>` and explain that this installs the hooks that capture
390	sessions and create checkpoints on commit. Mention `entire agent list`
391	to see what is supported. (`agent` and its `add` / `list` subcommands
392	must appear in the discovered surface; if they do not, fall back to
393	"Run `entire help` to see how to install agent hooks in your version.")
394	
395	**5. First-capture tour branch.** Entire is enabled and a supported
396	agent is installed, but there is not enough captured history yet to
397	demo the workflows concretely. Tell the user that checkpoints are
398	created automatically when their agent runs and commits, and that to
399	see the system work end-to-end they should:
400	- run a small task with their agent (any task, even "say hello in a
401	  comment"),
402	- commit the change,
403	- optionally run `entire login` if they want cloud search as part of
404	  the tour,
405	- come back and re-invoke this skill — there will be captured history
406	  to demo.
407	
408	Do not fabricate a checkpoint or run a placeholder task on the user's
409	behalf.
410	
411	**6. Workflow tour branch (the main path).** Render the goals → command
412	recipe. The lines below are the **template**; the actual rendered text
413	is built from the discovery output, so any goal whose required command
414	path is absent is dropped, any command path marked `requiresLogin`
415	gets a `(login required)` suffix, and any command marked
416	`isExperimental` gets an `(experimental)` suffix. The deeper-drill-down skill names in
417	parentheses are mentioned at most once each and only on the line where
418	the workflow already exists in the CLI:
419	
420	```text
421	Here is what your installed Entire CLI can do (entire <CLI version>):
422	
423	- Set up                  →  entire enable / entire agent add <name> / entire login
424	- See what's going on     →  entire status / entire session current / entire session list / entire activity
425	- Find prior work         →  entire checkpoint search "<query>" (login required)      (deeper: /search)
426	- Understand a change     →  entire checkpoint explain <id|sha>      (deeper: /explain, /what-happened)
427	- Undo / recover          →  entire checkpoint rewind  /  entire clean
428	- Summarize / share       →  entire dispatch --since 7d              (deeper: /session-handoff)
429	- Troubleshoot            →  entire doctor
430	
431	Other commands available in your CLI:
432	- <name>: <short description verbatim from `entire --help`>
433	- ...
434	```
435	
436	The skill prefers the canonical command spellings advertised in
437	`entire --help`. `entire search` exists today as a hidden alias of
438	`entire checkpoint search`; the rendered tour uses
439	`entire checkpoint search` because that is the form the live help
440	actually advertises. If a future CLI release promotes `search` to a
441	top-level command (it would then appear in `Available Commands`), the
442	discovery step picks that up and the rendered line uses the shorter
443	form on its own. Likewise, auth-gated commands are tagged from the live
444	help text for that exact command path, not from a hardcoded list.
445	
446	Then **demo exactly one** concrete Entire result, using a valid command
447	path in this order. Each candidate is only attempted if its command was
448	present in the discovery output:
449	
450	- If `entire auth status` says the user is logged in **and**
451	  `git config --get remote.origin.url` resolves to a GitHub repo slug,
452	  try a repo-scoped search first:
453	
454	```bash
455	entire checkpoint search "<current-repo-basename>" --repo <owner/name> --json --limit 3
456	```
457	
458	  If that search returns at least one hit, summarize the top hit as
459	  the demo and point out that `/search` is the skill for deeper use.
460	  If it returns zero hits, do **not** show an empty result; fall
461	  through to the local checkpoint / dispatch demo path below so the
462	  tour still ends with evidence from this repo.
463	
464	- Otherwise, if `entire checkpoint list --no-pager` shows at least one
465	  checkpoint on the current branch, expand the newest checkpoint with
466	  the current command spelling:
467	
468	```bash
469	entire checkpoint explain <checkpoint-id> --short --no-pager
470	```
471	
472	  Summarize that checkpoint as the demo and point out that `/explain`
473	  and `/what-happened` drill into the same captured history more
474	  precisely.
475	
476	- Otherwise, use the local cross-branch summary command:
477	
478	```bash
479	entire dispatch --local --all-branches --since 30d
480	```
481	
482	  Summarize one recent thread of work and frame it as "this is the
483	  captured history the deeper workflows search and explain."
484	
485	End with a one-liner that points at `docs.entire.io/cli` for deeper
486	docs and mentions that the focused skills (`/search`, `/explain`,
487	`/what-happened`, `/session-handoff`, `/session-to-skill`) drill into
488	the same workflows above when the user wants more than the CLI
489	recipes.

491	### Failure modes
492	
493	The skill must distinguish and report each of these states explicitly:
494	
495	- `entire` not installed → "The Entire CLI is required but not installed.
496	  Install it from https://docs.entire.io/cli/install and try again."
497	  No header.
498	- Current directory is not a git repo → "Entire works inside a git
499	  repository. Run `git init` first or `cd` into one, then re-invoke this
500	  skill." No header.
501	- `entire status` works but says disabled → enter the Setup tour branch
502	  (this is success, header applies).
503	- `entire login` required for cloud search → mention it in the tour but do
504	  not block local-only tours on it. Search is auth-gated; `status`,
505	  `session current`, `checkpoint list`, and `dispatch --local` still give the
506	  user value without logging in.
507	- Any other CLI error → report the exact error in one short excerpt and
508	  suggest re-running with `--help` or filing a `doctor` report
509	  (`entire doctor`). No header.

638	### Rollout
639	
640	`npx skills add https://github.com/entireio/skills --all` and the per-agent
641	install paths in the README pick up `skills/getting-started/` automatically
642	once the directory exists in `main`. There is no migration step, no flag, no
643	gating. Users who installed by cloning the repo into an agent discovery path
644	get the new skill on their next `git pull`; Gemini users get it on
645	`gemini extensions update entire`; Claude marketplace and other plugin users
646	get it on their normal plugin refresh / reinstall path. Users who pinned
647	OpenCode to a git tag only see the new skill after moving from `v0.3.0` to
648	`v0.4.0`, which is why `.opencode/INSTALL.md` updates in the same PR.

650	## Alternatives Considered
651	
652	1. **Expand the README "Quick Start" instead of adding a skill.** Rejected:
653	   the README is read on github.com by humans, not by the agent at invoke
654	   time. A new user typing "I'm new to Entire" into their agent never sees
655	   the README content unless the agent browses to it, and even then the
656	   agent answers in freeform prose rather than running the canonical
657	   commands. The whole point of this repo is to make Entire workflows
658	   agent-invokable.
659	
660	2. **One skill per onboarding stage** (`getting-started-setup`,
661	   `getting-started-first-checkpoint`, `getting-started-tour`). Rejected:
662	   the user does not know which stage they are in — that is exactly what
663	   they need the skill to detect for them. Splitting into three skills
664	   moves the dispatch problem onto the user.
665	
666	3. **Add a `entire tour` subcommand to the CLI.** Rejected: it would not
667	   integrate with the agent's response surface (no follow-up Q&A, no
668	   delegation to other skills), and it is a cross-repo change. The skills
669	   repo is the right home because the value is in the agent flow, not in a
670	   one-shot CLI output.
671	
672	4. **Make `getting-started` a thin wrapper that immediately delegates to
673	   `search` against the term "getting started".** Rejected: that produces
674	   results about *the user's* prior work on getting-started topics, not an
675	   introduction to Entire. Wrong abstraction.
676	
677	5. **Name it `tour` or revive `code-tour`.** Rejected: this repo already used
678	   `project-overview` / `code-tour` for repository onboarding. Reusing that
679	   vocabulary would blur "teach me Entire" with "teach me this codebase."
680	   `getting-started` keeps the new skill scoped to Entire itself while still
681	   listing `tour` as a trigger phrase in the description.

683	## Risks & Open Questions
684	
685	- **Trigger overlap with existing skills.** A user who says "search past
686	  work and explain what we did" should still get `search` first, not
687	  `getting-started`. Mitigation: the description text is explicit that this
688	  skill is for users who are *new* to Entire or want an overview, and the
689	  trigger phrases are intro-shaped ("I'm new to", "tour", "what can it
690	  do"). Skill priority falls out of trigger specificity; this is the same
691	  risk every other skill in this repo carries and has not been a problem
692	  in practice.
693	
694	- **Skill content drifts as the CLI evolves.** This is the central risk the
695	  reviewer flagged in round 1. Mitigation: the skill is **CLI-driven** —
696	  every command it teaches is parsed out of `entire --help` /
697	  `entire help <name>` at invocation time (Workflow step 2). The fixed
698	  parts of the skill body are the goal labels (`Set up`, `Find prior
699	  work`, …) and the goal-mapping table; the literal command names come
700	  from the live CLI, so the skill teaches whatever that CLI actually
701	  exposes. Verified against `entire 0.6.0` today: `Available Commands` =
702	  `activity, agent, auth, checkpoint, clean, configure, disable,
703	  dispatch, doctor, enable, help, login, logout, session, status,
704	  version`; `entire search` does not appear in that block (it is a
705	  hidden alias of `entire checkpoint search`), so the rendered tour
706	  uses the `checkpoint search` form. I also verified that
707	  `entire activity --json` and `entire checkpoint list --limit` do
708	  **not** exist, that `entire explain` is deprecated in favor of
709	  `entire checkpoint explain`, and that no command currently advertises
710	  `experimental`/`beta`/`alpha`/`preview`/`labs` in its help text — so
711	  the experimental-tagging code must handle the zero-match case
712	  gracefully.
713	
714	- **Branch-local checkpoint views can misclassify a repo with real history.**
715	  `entire checkpoint list` only reports checkpoints on the current branch. On
716	  this repo's `learn-entire-cli` branch it prints "No checkpoints found on
717	  this branch" even though `entire dispatch --local --all-branches --since 30d`
718	  returns recent Entire-backed work. Mitigation: branch-local checkpoint output
719	  is only one probe; the workflow gate also considers `session current` and
720	  local cross-branch dispatch.
721	
722	- **Demo step might not produce a useful result on small, fresh, or not-yet-indexed repos.**
723	  A logged-in user can still have zero remote hits if the repo lacks a GitHub
724	  `origin` or has not indexed checkpoints yet. Mitigation: remote search is
725	  repo-scoped and opportunistic; if it yields no hits, the Workflow tour branch
726	  falls back to checkpoint explain and then local dispatch summary. The
727	  First-capture branch handles the truly empty case explicitly. There is no
728	  path that ends with fabricated history or a dead-end empty search result.
729	
730	- **`entire status --json` behavior on older CLI versions.** Verified
731	  available in the currently installed CLI (`entire status --help` lists
732	  `--json`). Users on a CLI older than that flag will see a JSON parse
733	  error; the skill detects that and falls back to parsing the human-readable
734	  output. Implementation note for the PR, not a blocker.
735	
736	- **README drift hides or misstates shipped skills.** The current README is
737	  already missing `session-to-skill` from the rendered "Included skills"
738	  section even though the file exists in the tree. Mitigation: fix that drift
739	  in the same PR while adding `getting-started`; do not leave the public docs
740	  partially updated.
741	
742	- **README recording.** The other four user-facing skills each have a
743	  recording embedded under their README section. Adding a recording is out
744	  of scope for this PR (per Non-Goals); the README entry will not include
745	  one initially, and a follow-up can add the recording without touching the
746	  skill itself.
747	
748	- **`entire --help` parser brittleness.** The discovery step depends on the
749	  shape of cobra's `Available Commands:` block (two-space indented,
750	  `name<whitespace>short-description`). If a future CLI release changes
751	  that format, the parse degrades to "no commands found" and the skill
752	  cannot render the workflow tour. Mitigation: when discovery returns an
753	  empty command list but `entire version` succeeded, the skill falls back
754	  to telling the user "I could not parse `entire --help` on your machine —
755	  run `entire help` directly to see the current command list" and still
756	  prints the goal labels with no command recipes attached, so the skill
757	  fails open instead of silently inventing commands. The discovery code
758	  also tolerates extra columns and ANSI color codes by stripping them
759	  before splitting on whitespace.
760	
761	- **Version-bump collision with abandoned `v0.4.0` history.** `git log`
762	  shows an unmerged commit `3896d53 Add entire-powered commit-push-pr
763	  skill, bump to v0.4.0` on a side branch that never landed on `main`.
764	  Today's `main` is `0.3.0`. We are reusing the `0.4.0` label for this
765	  skill's release. Mitigation: nothing on `main` carries the `0.4.0`
766	  string, no tag named `v0.4.0` exists in the repo, and no published
767	  marketplace entry references `0.4.0`, so the reuse is safe — verify
768	  with `git tag --list 'v0.4.0'` (must be empty) and
769	  `git grep '"0.4.0"' main -- '*.json'` (must be empty) before tagging.
770	
771	## Decision
772	
773	Ship a single PR that adds `skills/getting-started/SKILL.md`, updates
774	`README.md` to advertise the guided tour and to bring the included-skills
775	list back into sync, bumps the six versioned packaging manifests to
776	`0.4.0`, and updates `.opencode/INSTALL.md`'s pinned tag example to
777	`v0.4.0`.
778	
779	The skill is **CLI-driven**: every workflow recipe it shows is built at
780	invocation time from `entire --help` and `entire help <name>`, so the
781	skill never teaches a command that does not exist on the user's
782	machine, and it picks up new commands automatically on the next CLI
783	release without a code change in this repo. It tags any command whose
784	help text matches `experimental`/`beta`/`alpha`/`preview`/`labs`
785	(case-insensitive, whole-word) so users can see what is stable vs. not.
786	It is **workflow-shaped**: discovered commands are mapped onto a small
787	fixed set of goals (Set up, See what's going on, Find prior work,
788	Understand a change, Undo / recover, Summarize / share, Troubleshoot)
789	via an explicit table, and any command that does not match a goal is
790	listed under "Other commands available in your CLI" rather than
791	silently hidden.
792	
793	State detection (git preflight, `entire version`,
794	`entire status --json`, `entire auth status`,
795	`entire session current --json`, `entire checkpoint list --no-pager`,
796	`entire dispatch --local --all-branches --since 30d`) routes the user
797	into one of four branches (Setup, Agent install, First-capture,
798	Workflow); the Workflow branch demos exactly one real Entire result.
799	The five focused skills (`/search`, `/explain`, `/what-happened`,
800	`/session-handoff`, `/session-to-skill`) are mentioned at most once
801	each as deeper drill-down hints attached to the matching workflow
802	goal — they are not the primary content.
803	
804	Validation runs the existing discovery command from `CONTRIBUTING.md`,
805	parse-checks `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json`, and runs eight manual
806	scenarios covering setup, agent install, empty-history,
807	cross-branch-history, logged-in search, header behavior, live-CLI
808	discovery against `entire 0.6.0`, and discovery resilience under a
809	stubbed future CLI surface. No CLI changes, no new dependencies, no

1	![skills cover](assets/gh-repo-cover.png)
2	
3	# skills
4	
5	Bring your Entire context to your agents with cross-agent skills.
6	
7	## Why This Exists
8	
9	The [Entire CLI](https://github.com/entireio/cli) captures the context behind your
10	code changes: prompts, transcripts,
11	[Checkpoints](https://docs.entire.io/cli/checkpoints), and the decisions that led
12	to each change, alongside your git history. This repository packages
13	agent-invokable workflows that teach coding agents how to use that context across
14	development environments.
15	
16	Instead of looking up Entire commands yourself, you can ask in plain language and
17	let your agent search prior work, inspect provenance, hand off session state, or
18	turn repeated workflows into new skills.
19	
20	## Install
21	
22	Install every skill with the [skills](https://skills.sh/) CLI:
23	
24	```bash
25	npx skills add https://github.com/entireio/skills --all
26	```
27	
28	Install one skill:
29	
30	```bash
31	npx skills add https://github.com/entireio/skills --skill search
32	```
33	
34	See [Agent-Specific Installation](#agent-specific-installation) for setup
35	instructions by agent.
36	
37	## Quick Start
38	
39	> [!NOTE]
40	> These skills are most useful in codebases with real
41	> [Checkpoints](https://docs.entire.io/cli/checkpoints) and session history. The
42	> richer the history, the more context your agent has to work with; in a new or
43	> lightly captured repository, some workflows may have less to find or explain.
44	
45	After installing, ask your agent for the workflow you want:
46	
47	```text
48	search past work for rate limiting
49	```
50	
51	```text
52	explain src/auth.ts
53	```
54	
55	```text
56	what happened here: src/auth.ts:42-57
57	```
58	
59	```text
60	hand off this session
61	```
62	
63	```text
64	turn my release notes workflow into a skill
65	```
66	
67	For a guided walkthrough, see the
68	[skills tutorial](https://docs.entire.io/skills/tutorial).
69	
70	## What skills help agents do
71	
72	| Goal | Example prompt |
73	| --- | --- |
74	| Find prior work before making changes | `search past work for the migration` |
75	| Understand the intent behind a function, file, or line | `explain parseConfig` |
76	| Investigate the latest change to a specific block | `what happened at src/auth.ts:42` |
77	| Pick up another agent's work | `hand off the codex session` |
78	| Convert repeated work into a reusable workflow | `make a skill from this session` |
79	
80	## Included skills
81	
82	The current repository includes these skills. Each skill lives in
83	`skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`.
84	
85	### `search`
86	
87	Finds prior work in your Entire history by topic, repo, branch, author, or time
88	window, so your agent can bring past context into the current task before making
89	changes.
90	
91	https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/52e50eae-ba53-4f31-ad2e-2a29a767a34f
92	
93	### `explain`
94	
95	Looks up the session behind a function, file, or line so your agent can explain
96	the requirement, decision, or original problem that shaped it.
97	
98	https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ab2e88f6-9bce-417b-ba71-6e93ecad370b
99	
100	### `what-happened`
101	
102	Starts from an exact file line, range, or pasted snippet and traces the latest
103	change with `git blame` and Checkpoint context. Useful when reviewing a concrete
104	block, debugging a regression, or asking why that block changed.
105	
106	https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/47db0daa-60bd-4000-b956-2d0e4d980b9b
107	
108	### `session-handoff`
109	
110	Reads saved or active session context so another agent can pick up the task
111	state, important discoveries, blockers, and next steps without making you
112	reconstruct everything manually.
113	
114	https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0df3b5cd-fe37-4145-af48-138642ccc8bc
115	
116	## Requirements
117	
118	These skills are designed for repositories where Entire has captured useful
119	history. Some workflows need:
120	
121	- the [Entire CLI](https://github.com/entireio/cli) installed
122	- a git repository with Entire sessions, Checkpoints, or Checkpoint-backed commits
123	- [`entire login`](https://docs.entire.io/cli/commands#login) for workflows that
124	  search indexed history
125	- a GitHub `origin` and pushed, indexed Checkpoints for remote search results
126	
127	Individual skills handle missing context differently and should explain what is
128	missing before falling back or stopping.
129	
130	## Agent-Specific Installation
131	
132	<!-- prettier-ignore-start -->
133

135	<summary>Claude Code</summary>
136	
137	```bash
138	/plugin marketplace add entireio/skills
139	/plugin install entire
140	```
141	
142	</details>
143	
144	<details>

151	```

1	---
2	name: search
3	description: Use when the user wants to find prior work, checkpoints, or agent conversations by topic, repo, branch, author, or recent time window
4	---
5	
6	# Search Checkpoints
7	
8	Use `entire search` to find relevant checkpoints before guessing from memory.
9	
10	## Response Format
11	
12	Begin the first response to this skill invocation with the line:
13	
14	`Entire Search:`
15	
16	followed by a blank line, then the content.
17	
18	- Apply the header to the **first response of the invocation only.** Do not re-print it on follow-up turns within the same invocation (e.g. after the user answers a clarifying question).
19	- Do **not** include the header on error or early-exit responses (e.g. "Entire CLI not installed", "authentication required", "no matches"). The header's presence should signal that the skill ran and produced real output.
20	
21	## When to Use
22	
23	- The user asks things like "have we done this before?", "search past work", "find the previous implementation", or "look for checkpoints about X"
24	- You need prior context from another branch, repo, author, or recent time period
25	- You want likely matches first, then a deeper transcript read only for the best hit
26	
27	Do not use this for the current active session. Use `session-handoff` for that.
28	
29	## Process
30	
31	1. Run a focused search with JSON output so results are easy to inspect:
32	
33	```bash
34	entire search "<query>" --json
35	```
36	
37	Add filters when the user already gave them or when the first search is too broad:
38	
39	```bash
40	entire search "<query>" --json --repo owner/name --branch branch-name --author "Name" --date week
41	```
42	
43	Inline filters are also supported in the query: `author:<name>`, `date:<week|month>`, `branch:<name>`, `repo:<owner/name>`, `repo:*`.
44	
45	2. Review the top matches and summarize the likely candidates for the user. Do not dump raw JSON unless they ask for it.
46	
47	3. If the user wants details on a specific result, open the checkpoint with:
48	
49	```bash
50	entire explain --checkpoint <checkpoint-id> --full --no-pager
51	```
52	
53	If `--full` fails, fall back to:
54	
55	```bash
56	entire explain --checkpoint <checkpoint-id> --raw-transcript --no-pager
57	```
58	
59	## Search Heuristics
60	
61	- Start with the user's domain terms, feature name, error text, file name, or ticket ID
62	- Prefer narrower searches before increasing `--limit`
63	- Add `--repo` or `repo:*` explicitly when repository scope matters
64	- If there are no useful hits, broaden in this order: remove branch filter, widen date, simplify query terms
65	
66	## Failure Modes
67	
68	- If search says authentication is required, tell the user to run `entire login`
69	- If there are no matches, say that clearly and mention the filters or query terms you tried
70	- If the user really wants the current session, switch to `session-handoff` instead of searching checkpoints
71

1	---
2	name: explain
3	description: Explains the intent behind source code by finding original session transcripts. Use explain with a function, file, or line of code to understand why it exists.
4	argument-hint: <function, file, or line>
5	---
6	
7	# Explain Intent
8	
9	Explain the intent behind source code by tracing it back to the original conversation where it was created. Works with:
10	
11	- **Functions** — Why does this function exist? What problem was it solving?
12	- **Files** — What's the purpose of this file? What requirements drove its creation?
13	- **Line changes** — Why was this specific line added or modified?
14	
15	## Response Format
16	
17	Begin the first response to this skill invocation with the line:
18	
19	`Entire Explain:`
20	
21	followed by a blank line, then the content.
22	
23	- Apply the header to the **first response of the invocation only.** Do not re-print it on follow-up turns within the same invocation (e.g. after the user answers a clarifying question).
24	- Do **not** include the header on error or early-exit responses (e.g. "Entire CLI is required but not installed", "this file is not tracked by git", "no session transcript was found for this commit"). The header's presence should signal that the skill ran and produced real output.
25	
26	## Process
27	
28	1. Verify the `entire` CLI is installed by running `entire version`.
29	   - If the command is not found, stop and tell the user: "The Entire CLI is required but not installed. Install it from https://entire.io/docs/cli and try again."
30	2. Use a Haiku agent to identify the commit that introduced the code via git blame or git log.
31	   - If the file is not tracked by git, stop and tell the user: "This file is not tracked by git, so I can't trace its history."
32	   - If git blame returns no useful result (e.g., the code is uncommitted), stop and tell the user: "This code hasn't been committed yet, so there's no history to trace."
33	3. Use a Sonnet agent to read the session transcript via `entire explain --no-pager --commit COMMIT_SHA`.
34	   - If the command fails or returns no transcript, stop and tell the user: "No session transcript was found for this commit. It may have been created outside of an Entire session (e.g., a manual commit)."
35

1	---
2	name: session-to-skill
3	description: Use when the user wants to turn one or more Entire-tracked sessions, checkpoints, or repeated agent workflows into a reusable agent skill.
4	---
5	
6	# Session To Skill
7	
8	Use this skill to help the user turn Entire session history into a focused skill draft.
9	
10	The goal is not to convert a whole transcript mechanically. Treat sessions and checkpoints as source material, then extract the reusable workflow the user wants to repeat.
11	
12	## Response Format
13	
14	Begin the first response to this skill invocation with the line:
15	
16	`Entire Session To Skill:`
17	
18	followed by a blank line, then the content.
19	
20	- Apply the header to the **first response of the invocation only.** Do not re-print it on follow-up turns within the same invocation.
21	- Do **not** include the header on error or early-exit responses, such as when Entire is not installed, the current directory is not a git repository, no relevant sessions are found, or the user has not identified what reusable behavior they want.
22	
23	## Rules
24	
25	1. First identify the reusable behavior the skill should capture. If the user has not said what the skill should help with, ask that question before reading transcripts.
26	2. Use Entire history as evidence. Prefer `entire search`, `entire session current`, session metadata files, and `entire explain` over asking the user to paste old transcripts.
27	3. A skill draft should be focused on future behavior, not a recap of the session. Preserve durable workflow, repo conventions, user corrections, commands, validation, and things to avoid.
28	4. When several sessions may be relevant, summarize the repeated workflow pattern, recommend a source set, and ask the user to confirm before expanding transcripts.
29	5. Do not write, install, or overwrite a skill file unless the user explicitly approves the destination. By default, present the `SKILL.md` draft in the response.
30	6. Do not include secrets, private credentials, raw logs, or unnecessary transcript detail in the generated skill.
31	
32	## Workflow
33	
34	### 1. Clarify The Skill Target
35	
36	If the user gives a clear target, continue. Examples:
37	
38	- "turn my blog publishing workflow into a skill"
39	- "make a skill from session 019..."
40	- "I keep doing release note drafting; make that reusable"
41	
42	If the target is vague, ask:
43	
44	```text
45	What should this skill help you do repeatedly?
46	```
47	
48	If the user wants a specific skill name, use it. Otherwise infer a short hyphen-case name from the target, then confirm it before writing files.
49	
50	### 2. Infer The Repeated Pattern
51	
52	If the user gives a checkpoint ID, skip to checkpoint expansion.
53	
54	If the user gives a session ID, read the matching session metadata from:
55	
56	```text
57	.git/entire-sessions/<session-id>.json
58	```
59	
60	If the user describes a repeated workflow but does not give a session or checkpoint, search Entire history with terms from the target:
61	
62	```bash
63	entire search "<workflow terms>" --json
64	```
65	
66	Use repo, branch, author, or date filters when the user provides them:
67	
68	```bash
69	entire search "<workflow terms>" --json --repo owner/name --branch branch-name --author "Name" --date month
70	```
71	
72	Interpret search results carefully:
73	
74	- If `entire search` returns valid JSON with `"total": 0` or an empty `results` array, do **not** call it an authentication failure. Say no indexed matches were found, then fall back to local session metadata.
75	- Only say authentication is required if the command output explicitly says authentication, login, or credentials are required.
76	- If search fails for any other reason, report the short error and fall back to local session metadata when available.
77	
78	When falling back locally, inspect `.git/entire-sessions/*.json` and match against `last_prompt`, `description`, `files_touched`, `started_at`, `last_interaction_time`, `agent_type`, and the user's workflow terms.
79	
80	Review the top results and infer the repeated workflow pattern before showing raw session choices. Lead with the pattern, not the IDs.
81	
82	Present:
83	
84	- the repeated workflow you think the user wants to capture
85	- the strongest source set you recommend using
86	- what each source contributes in plain language, such as "core workflow", "image handling", "validation", or "copy-editing pattern"
87	- any sessions you plan to ignore because they look metadata-only, duplicate, or one-off
88	
89	Keep session IDs and checkpoint IDs as supporting details, not the main decision surface. Ask the user to confirm the pattern and source set before expanding detailed transcripts.
90	
91	Example:
92	
93	```text
94	I found a repeated workflow: publishing blog posts in entire.io from drafts, using repo-specific front matter, slugged asset folders, user-provided images, and website checks.
95	
96	I recommend using the strongest matching sessions as source material:
97	- core workflow: <session-id>
98	- image handling: <session-id>
99	- validation mechanics: <session-id>
100	
101	I will ignore metadata-only or one-off edit sessions unless you want them included. Should I continue with this source set?
102	```
103	
104	### 3. Read Source Material
105	
106	For a checkpoint, run:
107	
108	```bash
109	entire explain --checkpoint <checkpoint-id> --full --no-pager
110	```
111	
112	If full output fails and the user wants more detail, fall back to:
113	
114	```bash
115	entire explain --checkpoint <checkpoint-id> --raw-transcript --no-pager
116	```
117	
118	For an active or current session, prefer:
119	
120	```bash
121	entire session current
122	```
123	
124	If the installed Entire CLI does not support the singular `session` group yet, use the session metadata fallback directly: inspect `.git/entire-sessions/*.json`, pick the relevant session by `last_interaction_time`, `started_at`, `agent_type`, or the user's requested agent, then extract `transcript_path`.
125	
126	When reading a raw transcript, extract relevant conversation and tool-call lines without dumping them to the user:
127	
128	```bash
129	grep -E '"type":"(message|function_call|user|assistant)"' <transcript_path> | cut -c1-2000
130	```
131	
132	For large transcripts, inspect the first prompts and final state first:
133	
134	```bash
135	grep -E '"type":"(message|function_call|user|assistant)"' <transcript_path> | head -40 | cut -c1-2000
136	grep -E '"type":"(message|function_call|user|assistant)"' <transcript_path> | tail -160 | cut -c1-2000
137	```
138	
139	If the session metadata lists files touched, inspect only files needed to understand durable conventions. Avoid broad repo exploration unless the skill target requires it.
140	
141	### 4. Extract Durable Lessons
142	
143	Before drafting, privately identify:
144	
145	- the repeated goal the future skill should accomplish
146	- triggers that should activate the skill
147	- required inputs the future agent should ask for
148	- repo-specific paths, file formats, front matter, naming, or asset placement
149	- commands and checks that proved the workflow worked
150	- user corrections and preferences from the session
151	- failed approaches or behaviors to avoid
152	- what was one-off and should not go into the skill
153	
154	If multiple sessions were selected, combine only the repeated or clearly reusable lessons. Do not average contradictory instructions; ask the user to choose when sessions disagree.
155	
156	### 5. Draft The Skill
157	
158	Create a complete `SKILL.md` draft with required front matter:
159	
160	```markdown
161	---
162	name: <hyphen-case-skill-name>
163	description: Use when <specific trigger and task>.
164	---
165	```
166	
167	The body should include:
168	
169	- a short purpose statement
170	- clear rules or guardrails
171	- a step-by-step workflow
172	- exact commands only when they are part of the reusable behavior
173	- expected outputs and validation steps
174	- failure handling or when to ask the user
175	
176	Keep the skill concise. Do not include the session recap, full transcript excerpts, checkpoint IDs, or implementation notes unless they are essential to future use.
177	
178	### 6. Deliver And Offer Installation
179	
180	Present the `SKILL.md` draft first unless the user already gave an explicit write path.
181	
182	After presenting the draft, ask whether the user wants it installed globally. Recommend the cross-agent path:
183	
184	```text
185	~/.agents/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md
186	```
187	
188	Use this install prompt shape:
189	
190	```text
191	Do you want me to install this skill globally?
192	
193	Recommended:
194	- Cross-agent: ~/.agents/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md
195	
196	Other options:
197	- Codex only: ~/.codex/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md
198	- Write to a repo-local draft: skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md
199	- Leave as draft only
200	```
201	
202	Only write files after the user chooses a destination. If the destination already exists, ask before overwriting it.
203	
204	Do not create symlinks unless the user explicitly asks for a development-linked install. If they ask for a symlink, explain the source and target paths before creating it.
205	
206	After writing a skill file, summarize:
207	
208	- where it was written
209	- which session(s) or checkpoint(s) informed it
210	- any assumptions or open questions
